context_update.summary: This chunk introduces a study on five low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (S1–S5) with varying C, Al, Ti, and Ni contents. The abstract reports bainitic transformation times: S1 at 350°C shows 1600 s, S3 with 8.08 wt.% Al requires 14,000–43,000 s due to stabilized retained austenite from C-enriched polygonal ferrite, and S5 (0.60% C, 3.92% Al) achieves 700–1000 s with accelerated kinetics from lenticular martensite near Ms. UTS ranges from 1315 to 1600 MPa; Ti-microalloyed S4 attains 10% average total elongation. Precipitate analysis reveals (Fe,M)xCy and TiMoC. The introduction reviews Al effects on density, phase stability, and prior nB steel performances. Section 2.1 details alloy design: S1–S4 are high-C (0.9–1.0 wt.%), high-Al (5.8–8 wt.%) with Mo and B; S4 reduces Al and C, adds Ti and 0.78% Ni; S5 uses 0.6% C, 3.92% Al, and 0.78% Ni. Full compositions are in Table 1. Later chunks will present heat treatment cycles, detailed kinetics, tensile data, and microstructural characterization.
mechanisms: C-enrichment around polygonal ferrite leading to excessive retained austenite stabilization
lenticular martensite near Ms accelerates transformation
Al suppresses carbide precipitation and accelerates bainitic transformation

context_update.summary: This chunk details the alloy design rationale for five low-density nano-bainitic steels (S1–S5), sample preparation, and characterization methods. High Al (5.8–8 wt.%) and limited Mn (1 wt.%) stabilize ferrite and accelerate transformation in S1–S4, requiring 0.9–1.0 wt.% C to maintain Ms ~300°C. S4 (0.87C, 5.76Al) introduces Ti microalloying and 0.78 wt.% Ni to suppress polygonal ferrite and enhance bainitic transformation. S5 uses a leaner composition (0.6C, 3.92Al) with 0.78 wt.% Ni for αp-free microstructures. Table 1 gives full chemical compositions. Processing includes vacuum arc melting, homogenization at 1100°C/24 h, hot rolling to ~11 mm, water-jet cutting, and dilatometric heat treatments in a DIL 805A; TA and Ms are determined by dilatometry and MatCalc, with TB set just above Ms and cooling at 100 K/s. S4 receives an additional 1250°C step to dissolve MX C-type precipitates. Bt is defined from the RLC plateau (Fig. 1). Microscopy methods: LOM (6% Nital, quantitative αp only for S3 at 200×, 5 regions), SEM/EDX (bainite thickness via 100 measurements/10 images, stereological correction Lt(t)=2Lt/π), EBSD (20 kV, 0.2 μm step), XRD with Rietveld refinement (ASTM E975-13, Vγ corrected for Vc via Eq. 1, Fig. 2). MatCalc precipitation modeling considers fcc, bcc, κ-carbide, hcp carbide, AlN, TiCN-TiMoC, with κ-carbide fixed due to spinodal decomposition. No mechanical property or transformation kinetic data are presented here; those results appear in later chunks. Key figures: Fig. 1(a) heat treatment cycle, Fig. 1(b) Bt determination, Fig. 2 Vγ methodology. Table 1 provides compositions.
processing_route: Hot rolling to ~11 mm, furnace cooling
Austenitization at TA > calculated values, 100 K/s cooling to TB
Austempering at TB just above Ms
key_variables: Al content (5.8–8 wt.% S1–S4; 3.92 wt.% S5)
C content (0.9–1.0 wt.% S1–S4; 0.6 wt.% S5)
microstructure: S3 exhibits higher polygonal ferrite fraction
κ-carbide assumed spinodal; TiMoC, (Fe,M)xCy carbides modeled
Bainitic ferrite lamellae thickness measured via stereology
mechanisms: Ni suppresses polygonal ferrite formation
Ti microalloying enhances bainitic transformation and mechanical properties
κ-carbide forms via spinodal decomposition, not classical nucleation
High Al and Si suppress carbide precipitation
unresolved_terms: MX C-type precipitates (precise stoichiometry not given)

context_update.summary: This chunk (Section 3.1) determines austenitizing temperatures (TA) and martensite start (Ms) temperatures for five low-density Al–C steels (S1–S5). Due to broadened dilation signals in high-C–Al grades, operative TA values were extracted from differentiated RLC curves, yielding 1062 °C (S1), 1031 °C (S2), 1067 °C (S4), ~900 °C (S5), and a broadened transition for S3. MatCalc simulations provided ferrite-free TA and carbide-dissolution temperatures. LOM at 1050 °C and higher temperatures quantified polygonal ferrite (αp) area fractions: S3 showed ~14.6% αp at 1050 °C and ~8.2% at 1220 °C, while S4 and S5 exhibited trace levels (≲1%) at 1150 °C. From dilatometry, simulations, and LOM, optimized austenitization was fixed at 1150 °C for S1, S2, S4, S5 and 1220 °C for S3 to suppress αp and ensure carbide dissolution. S4 may receive an extra 1250 °C step to dissolve MC/TiC precipitates. Ms values were measured (S1: 277, S2: 274, S3: 258, S4: 305/310, S5: 331 °C), and austempering temperatures were set slightly above Ms. The chunk also references Fig. 2 for retained austenite volume fraction determination, Fig. 3–5 for TA, LOM, and Ms data, and Table 2 summarizing TA and Ms. Remaining unresolved: αp-free condition not achieved for S3, and transformation kinetics not yet presented.
processing_route: Austenitization at 1150 °C for S1, S2, S4, S5
Optional 1250 °C for S4 to dissolve MC/TiC
key_variables: TA MatCalc ferrite-free: S1 991°C, S2 929°C, S3 1177°C, S4 1016°C, S5 986°C
αp area fraction at 1050°C: S3 ~14.6%, at 1220°C: ~8.2%
αp trace ≲1% at 1150°C for S4 and S5
Carbide dissolution Tcd: S1-S2 1103-1107°C, S4 ~1134°C, S5 ~775°C
mechanisms: Carbon-enriched austenite lowers Ms
High Al raises Ms
κ-carbide dissolution and ferrite retention lead to carbon partitioning and lowest Ms in S3
S5 high Ms due to low effective carbon
Austempering above Ms avoids martensite
unresolved_terms: Fully αp-free condition for S3 not achieved

context_update.summary: Section 3.2 presents bainitic transformation kinetics for S1–S5 from dilatometric RLC–time curves (Fig. 6). S1 and S2 (Al≥6%, C≈0.9–1.0%) transformed at 350°C in 1600 s and 2300 s, and at 300°C in 4000 s and 3600 s, respectively; S1 had systematically shorter incubations. S3 (8.08% Al, Ms 258°C) showed extremely slow kinetics: ~14,000 s at 350°C and ~43,000 s at 270°C, with a prolonged slow growth stage, indicating that high Al does not accelerate bainite formation in this dual-phase condition. S4 (Ti-microalloyed, Ni-containing) had the shortest incubation (50–60 s) and completed transformation in 2600–3500 s depending on TA/TB. S5 (0.60% C, 3.92% Al, Ms 331°C) exhibited incubation within 10 s and completion in 700–1000 s, outperforming literature 3% and 5% Al steels and approaching near-stoichiometric optimum. The chunk connects kinetics to compositional and microstructural factors that will be discussed in later sections (3.4.3–3.4.4).
material_systems: Low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels: S1, S2, S3, S4, S5
microstructure: polygonal ferrite (αp) negligible in S4 and S5
mechanisms: Low Ms delays bainite transformation (S3)
High Al (>6%) does not guarantee acceleration, especially with dual-phase (S3)
Ti microalloying accelerates transformation but is limited by bainitic sheaf impingement (S4)
Near-stoichiometric Al–C composition optimizes transformation kinetics (S5)
Lower C content reduces austenite stability and accelerates bainite transformation (S5)
0.78% Ni suppresses polygonal ferrite without impairing kinetics
unresolved_terms: dual-phase microstructure of S3 (to be discussed in 3.4.3)
role of sheaf impingement in S4

S3 (8.08% Al, Ms 258°C) showed extremely slow kinetics: ~14,000 s at 350°C and ~43,000 s at 270°C, with a prolonged slow growth stage, indicating that high Al does not accelerate bainite formation in this dual-phase condition. S4 (Ti-microalloyed, Ni-containing) had the shortest incubation (50–60 s) and completed transformation in 2600–3500 s depending on TA/TB. S5 (0.60% C, 3.92% Al, Ms 331°C) exhibited incubation within 10 s and completion in 700–1000 s, outperforming literature 3% and 5% Al steels and approaching near-stoichiometric optimum. The chunk connects kinetics to compositional and microstructural factors that will be discussed in later sections (3.4.3–3.4.4).
Material systems: Low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels; Fe-C-Al-Si-Mn-Mo-(B) system; Ti-microalloyed steel; Ni-containing steel; Ti-microalloyed steel (S4); Ni-containing steels (S4, S5); S1–S5 grades; κ-carbide
Sample aliases: S1; S2; S3; S4; S5
Processing route: Vacuum arc melting; Homogenization: 1100 °C, 24 h, Ar; Hot rolling to ~11 mm, furnace cooling; Water-jet cutting; DIL 805A dilatometer heat treatments; Austenitization at TA > calculated values, 100 K/s cooling to TB; Austempering at TB just above Ms; Additional 1250 °C treatment for S4
Key variables: Al content (5.8–8 wt.%); C content (0.6–1.0 wt.%); Ti microalloying; Ni addition (0.78%); austempering temperature; transformation time; UTS; total elongation
Microstructure: polygonal ferrite (αp); retained austenite (γr); lenticular martensite; bainite; (Fe,M)xCy precipitates; TiMoC precipitates; Target: αp-free nano-bainite + retained austenite; S3 exhibits higher polygonal ferrite fraction
Properties: UTS (1315–1600 MPa); total elongation (10% for S4); yield strength trends; Ms temperatures; bainitic transformation kinetics; incubation time; bainite finish time
Mechanisms: C-enrichment around polygonal ferrite leading to excessive retained austenite stabilization; lenticular martensite near Ms accelerates transformation; Al suppresses carbide precipitation and accelerates bainitic transformation; Ni suppresses polygonal ferrite formation; Ti microalloying enhances bainitic transformation and mechanical properties; κ-carbide forms via spinodal decomposition, not classical nucleation; High Al and Si suppress carbide precipitation; Carbon-enriched austenite lowers Ms
Figures or tables: Table 1; Fig. 1(a); Fig. 1(b); Fig. 2; Fig. 3; Fig. 4; Fig. 5; Table 2

context_update.summary: This chunk completes transformation kinetics for S5 (section 3.2), showing fastest kinetics (700–1000 s) and near-stoichiometric optimum. Section 3.3 presents mechanical properties (Fig. 7): hardness 450–500 HV30, maximum Rp0.2 1225 MPa (S1, 300 °C), minimum 945 MPa (S3, 270 °C). S1/S2 show similar properties; 0.1 wt.% C difference negligible. S3 at 270 °C suffers ductility drop to 2.4% (PSE ~3480 MPa·%). S4 with Ti+Ni achieves best balance (El up to 10%, PSE up to 13600 MPa·%). S5 intermediate between 3% and 5% Al literature values. Section 3.4.1 provides quantitative microstructure (Table 3): Vγ up to 34% at high TB in high-Al steels, <30% in S4/S5; Lt(t) 34–68 nm, unaffected by TA in S4; pronounced strain partitioning in S3 at 270 °C (εγ 0.54% vs εα 0.33%) explains low elongation. Summary mechanisms: TB controls plate thickness and γr stability; 8.08% Al harms low-TB ductility; C variation secondary; Ti+Ni improves elongation and PSE. Subsequent sections will address further microscopy and precipitation.
mechanisms: TB controls UTS–El trade-off via plate thickness and retained austenite stability
8.08 wt.% Al (S3) is detrimental to ductility at low TB
C variations near 1 wt.% are secondary to Al level in setting the balance
Ti+Ni alloying in S4 compensates for lower C by elevating elongation and PSE
Strain partitioning in S3 at 270 °C causes early localization and ductility loss to 2.4%

context_update.summary: This chunk (sections 3.4.1–3.4.3) presents quantitative microstructural analysis and TTP precipitation characteristics for low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels. Micro-strain measurements show similar ε values (0.44–0.47%) for bainitic ferrite and retained austenite in S2 and S5, while S1 and S4 at TB=350°C exhibit higher εα (0.30–0.45%). In S3 at 270°C, pronounced strain partitioning (εγ 0.54% vs εα 0.33%) leads to poor elongation (2.4%). TTP diagrams (Fig. 8) reveal (Fe,M)xCy precipitation during cooling between 700–1150°C in S1/S3 and 700–1000°C in S5; S4 nucleation is suppressed above 950°C by Ti–Mo–C counter-coarsening. Higher Al in S3 slightly increases precipitate fraction with negligible mechanical impact. Discussion on ≥6% Al steels (S1–S3) finds spherical precipitates without micro-voids, EDX-detected Si and Mo, no observable κ-carbide due to low Mn, and increased Lt(t) standard deviation at 350°C producing interlocked bainitic morphologies. In S3, 8.2% αp causes C-partitioning, sluggish γr, and requires 43,200 s for near-complete transformation, explaining low elongation. These microstructural-mechanical linkages set the stage for upcoming discussions on S4 and S5.
processing_route: S1 and S4 at TA=1150°C and TB=320/350°C
properties: Bainitic plate thickness (58 nm → 40 nm in S3 at 270°C)
mechanisms: Strain partitioning between γr and bainitic ferrite lowers elongation at low TB
Spherical (Fe,M)xCy precipitates do not initiate micro-voids, negligible effect on ductility
Si entrapment during rapid cooling suppresses (Fe,M)xCy, Mo stabilizes them
C-partitioning from αp stabilizes austenite and slows bainitic transformation
Lower alloying (S4, S5) suppresses Lt(t) variation, improving elongation

context_update.summary: This chunk discussed the microstructural mechanisms behind delayed bainitic transformation in high-Al steels (S3), the role of TiMoC precipitates in S4, and the temperature-dependent microstructure–property relations in the low C–Al steel S5. In S3, pre-existing polygonal ferrite (αp) caused strong C-partitioning that locally stabilised austenite and hindered bainite growth, requiring 43,200 s for near-complete transformation at 270 °C; abnormal strain partitioning (εγ 0.33%, εα 0.58%) led to brittle boundaries and poor ductility. For S4, Ti–Mo–C complex carbides suppressed (Fe,M)xCy and MoC, exhibited a longitudinal alignment from post-casting cooling and hot deformation, and were free of micro-defects. An austenitisation temperature of 1250 °C reduced impingement from fine prior austenite grains. S4 achieved the best elongation (10–12%) at TA=1150 °C, TB=350 °C. In S5, austempering at 335 °C produced fine bainite with sporadic martensite, high micro-strains (εγ 0.51%, εα 0.45%), low γr (19%), UTS 1530 MPa and El 7%; raising TB to 365 °C coarsened the bainite, eliminated martensite, increased γr to ~26%, converged micro-strains (0.42–0.43%), and gave UTS 1315 MPa, El 8.7%. KAM analysis at two magnifications highlighted local curvature near martensite. No (Fe,M)xCy was observed in S5. Evidence relied heavily on Figs. 9–11 and Table 3.
mechanisms: C-partitioning from polygonal ferrite stabilizes austenite and inhibits bainite growth
TiMoC suppresses (Fe,M)xCy and MoC, provides nucleation sites
Austenitization at 1250 °C reduces impingement
Stress-assisted martensite formation above Ms
TB controls γr fraction and KAM balance for strength–ductility
